model_output_raw_text: {"declared_mode":"layout","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"BASE_CHANNELS = 16","old":"BASE_CHANNELS = 8","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""},{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"FC_HIDDEN = 64","old":"FC_HIDDEN = 32","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"layout","rationale":"The current network is extremely small for a 256-step CIFAR-10 budget. A modest width increase is a simple way to raise representation capacity while keeping the training loop and optimization setup unchanged, which is a good trade if the current bottleneck is underfitting rather than optimization instability.","secondary_modes":["topk"]}

{"declared_mode":"micro","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"BATCH_SIZE = 128","old":"BATCH_SIZE = 64","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"micro","rationale":"The verifier is fixed-step rather than fixed-epoch, so the current run only exposes the model to 16,384 training examples. Increasing batch size is a simple way to cover substantially more of CIFAR-10 within the same 256 updates, which is a good next test now that the wider model is still clearly undertrained.","secondary_modes":["layout","topk"]}

{"declared_mode":"topk","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"LEARNING_RATE = 2e-3","old":"LEARNING_RATE = 1.5e-3","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"topk","rationale":"After doubling batch size, the optimizer is likely conservative relative to the larger effective sample count per update. A modest LR increase is the simplest next test to improve early progress without changing the now-successful architecture or schedule shape.","secondary_modes":["micro","summaries"]}

{"declared_mode":"caching","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"    criterion = nn.CrossEntropyLoss(label_smoothing=0.1)","old":"    criterion = nn.CrossEntropyLoss()","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"caching","rationale":"The current run is learning quickly and may now be paying a calibration penalty in validation cross-entropy. Label smoothing is a low-complexity regularization change that often improves validation loss directly, which matches the checker objective better than chasing accuracy alone.","secondary_modes":["topk"]}

{"declared_mode":"layout","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"DEPTH = 2","old":"DEPTH = 1","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""},{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"            ResidualBlock(channels, dropout=DROPOUT_RATE, use_bn=USE_BATCHNORM),
            ResidualBlock(channels, dropout=DROPOUT_RATE, use_bn=USE_BATCHNORM),
","old":"            ResidualBlock(channels, dropout=DROPOUT_RATE, use_bn=USE_BATCHNORM),
","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"layout","rationale":"The recent gains suggest the current setup is still capacity-limited more than optimization-limited. Adding a second residual block is a compact way to deepen the feature extractor without changing width, batch size, or the now-working optimizer schedule.","secondary_modes":["caching"]}

{"declared_mode":"layout","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"DEPTH = 3","old":"DEPTH = 2","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""},{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"            ResidualBlock(channels, dropout=DROPOUT_RATE, use_bn=USE_BATCHNORM),
            ResidualBlock(channels, dropout=DROPOUT_RATE, use_bn=USE_BATCHNORM),
            ResidualBlock(channels, dropout=DROPOUT_RATE, use_bn=USE_BATCHNORM),
","old":"            ResidualBlock(channels, dropout=DROPOUT_RATE, use_bn=USE_BATCHNORM),
            ResidualBlock(channels, dropout=DROPOUT_RATE, use_bn=USE_BATCHNORM),
","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"layout","rationale":"Depth has produced the largest recent gain, which suggests the model is still feature-limited despite the higher batch size and improved optimizer setup. Extending the same residual stack by one more block is the most direct follow-up and preserves the currently successful training dynamics.","secondary_modes":["caching"]}

{"declared_mode":"indexing","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"ADAM_BETAS = (0.9, 0.99)","old":"ADAM_BETAS = (0.9, 0.999)","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"indexing","rationale":"With only 256 optimizer steps, AdamW's default second-moment timescale is fairly long. Lowering beta2 is a simple way to make the adaptive statistics respond faster in this short-budget regime without disturbing the architecture or scheduler that currently work best.","secondary_modes":["topk"]}

{"declared_mode":"indexing","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"ADAM_BETAS = (0.9, 0.999)","old":"ADAM_BETAS = (0.9, 0.99)","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"indexing","rationale":"The lower beta2 regressed relative to the current best setup, so the cleanest next step is to restore the original AdamW moments and recover the stronger baseline before exploring other axes.","secondary_modes":["micro"]}

{"declared_mode":"micro","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"BATCH_SIZE = 192","old":"BATCH_SIZE = 128","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"micro","rationale":"The strongest gains so far came from giving the model more data coverage within the fixed 256-step budget. Moving from 128 to 192 gets this deeper network close to a full pass over CIFAR-10 while leaving the successful optimizer and architecture otherwise unchanged.","secondary_modes":["topk","layout"]}

{"declared_mode":"micro","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"BATCH_SIZE = 224","old":"BATCH_SIZE = 192","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"micro","rationale":"The largest improvements have come from increasing data coverage under the fixed 256-step budget. Since 192 was a strong positive move, a smaller follow-up increase to 224 is a reasonable next probe to capture a bit more of the dataset without changing the optimizer or architecture that are now working well.","secondary_modes":["topk"]}

{"declared_mode":"micro","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"BATCH_SIZE = 256","old":"BATCH_SIZE = 224","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"micro","rationale":"Batch size has been the most reliable lever because the verifier is fixed-step and larger batches keep increasing effective data coverage. Pushing to 256 is the natural next step: it gives the model a full-epoch-scale view of the data within budget while preserving the best-performing architecture and optimizer settings.","secondary_modes":["topk"]}

{"declared_mode":"micro","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"BATCH_SIZE = 224","old":"BATCH_SIZE = 256","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"micro","rationale":"The jump from 224 to 256 slightly regressed, so the best-supported move is to restore the 224 batch size that gave the strongest visible result. That keeps the effective data coverage high without crossing into the less effective regime seen at 256.","secondary_modes":["topk"]}

{"declared_mode":"topk","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"LEARNING_RATE = 2.2e-3","old":"LEARNING_RATE = 2e-3","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"topk","rationale":"Batch size has increased substantially since the last learning-rate sweep, so the current LR may now be slightly conservative. A small increase is the simplest way to test whether the 224-batch regime can make better use of its larger effective batch without disturbing the rest of the best-performing setup.","secondary_modes":["micro"]}

{"declared_mode":"topk","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"LEARNING_RATE = 2.4e-3","old":"LEARNING_RATE = 2.2e-3","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"topk","rationale":"The move from 2.0e-3 to 2.2e-3 was modestly positive, so the next efficient probe is to continue in the same direction with another small step up. This keeps the best-performing batch size and architecture fixed while testing whether the larger-batch regime still wants a slightly higher step size.","secondary_modes":["micro"]}

{"declared_mode":"topk","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"LEARNING_RATE = 2.2e-3","old":"LEARNING_RATE = 2.4e-3","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"topk","rationale":"The higher learning rate regressed, so the cleanest next step is to restore the 2.2e-3 setting that produced the current best visible result before using the remaining budget on a different axis.","secondary_modes":["micro"]}

{"declared_mode":"caching","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"WEIGHT_DECAY = 2e-4","old":"WEIGHT_DECAY = 1e-4","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"caching","rationale":"The run is now seeing much more of the dataset than earlier, so a slightly stronger regularization penalty is a reasonable late-stage probe. Increasing weight decay is a low-complexity change that can improve validation loss without disturbing the architecture, batch size, or schedule that currently define the best setup.","secondary_modes":["topk"]}

{"declared_mode":"caching","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"WEIGHT_DECAY = 3e-4","old":"WEIGHT_DECAY = 2e-4","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"caching","rationale":"The first increase in weight decay was mildly positive, which suggests the current larger-batch, near-full-epoch regime can use a bit more regularization. With one step left, the most coherent follow-up is a small continuation in the same direction rather than a broader structural change.","secondary_modes":["topk"]}
